project.name: jobsync
project.description: JobSync is a free, open-source, self-hosted web app for tracking and organizing job applications, with EURES/ESCO integration for European job discovery, AI resume review and job matching. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.name: 029-scope-enrichment-cache-keys-by-userid.md

layers.description: Project build tooling and configuration — package.json, tsconfig, Next.js/Tailwind/PostCSS/Jest/Playwright configs, devenv, Prisma config, and the ./scripts dev/build/test/prisma helper scripts.

layers.name: Documentation & Specifications
layers.description: Markdown documentation (README, CONTRIBUTING, docs/, conductor track docs) and authoritative Allium domain specifications (specs/*.allium) that govern domain rules.

layers.name: Data Layer
layers.description: Prisma persistence layer — schema.prisma, the SQLite migration history (migrations/*.sql tables), and the database seed — defining the relational model for jobs, automations, CRM, and notifications.

layers.name: Application & Domain Layer
layers.description: Server-action Repositories (src/actions), domain models/value objects (src/models), and supporting library services — events, scheduler, notifications, auth, PII redaction, assets, email/push, public-API helpers — implementing JobSync business logic.

layers.name: Presentation Layer
layers.description: React UI — Shadcn components, staging/kanban/crm/automations/settings/profile views, hooks, context, i18n dictionaries, Next.js page routes/layouts, and client assets (service worker).

layers.name: API & Route Handlers
layers.description: Next.js route handlers — the public versioned /api/v1 surface and internal /api/* proxies (ESCO/EURES, scheduler SSE, logos, auth) plus the edge middleware that gates requests.

layers.name: Infrastructure Layer
layers.description: Containerization and deployment — multi-stage Dockerfiles, docker-compose, entrypoint/deploy/release shell scripts, and the GitHub Actions CI pipeline.

layers.name: Connector / Integration ACL
layers.description: The Connector/Module anti-corruption layer (src/lib/connector) — unified module registry, resilience, job-discovery/AI-provider/data-enrichment/reference-data connectors and their EURES/Arbeitsagentur/JSearch/Ollama/ESCO/holiday modules.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to learn what JobSync is: a self-hosted job application tracker built on Next.js 15, Prisma/SQLite, and Shadcn UI, with EURES/ESCO European job discovery and AI matching via Ollama/DeepSeek. It covers the Docker quick-start, the feature set, and contribution pointers, giving you the mental model the rest of the tour fills in. Keep its architecture description in mind as we descend through each layer.

tour.title: Application Entry & Middleware
tour.description: The Next.js App Router root layout is the runtime entry point: it wraps the whole tree with the locale provider, theme provider, error-capture init, and global styles. Every request first passes through the edge middleware, which strips credential params from auth URLs, applies security headers, and runs the NextAuth handler. Together these two files define how a request is bootstrapped and secured before any page renders.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router uses nested layout.tsx files as composition boundaries — a root layout wraps all routes, and middleware.ts runs at the edge before any route handler, making it the natural place for cross-cutting security concerns.

tour.title: Internationalization Backbone
tour.description: i18n is the single most depended-upon module in the codebase — the client barrel (`@/i18n`) has the highest fan-in of any file, and the server barrel mirrors it for Server Components and actions. Both expose translation hooks/functions plus locale-aware date and number formatters behind an adapter pattern, so consumers never import internal dictionaries directly. Understanding this barrel split (client vs. server-only) explains an import convention you will see in nearly every UI and action file.
tour.languageLesson: The adapter/barrel pattern re-exports a stable public surface (`@/i18n`) so the underlying dictionary implementation can be swapped (e.g. to LinguiJS) without touching the hundreds of consumer files that import from it.

tour.title: Presentation Layer in Action
tour.description: With the layout and providers understood, look at a representative feature surface: the dashboard layout wires the header, sidebar, toaster, activity context, and undo listener around feature pages, while the staging container is one of the richest client components, coordinating deck/list modes, deck-action routing through a state machine, bulk actions, and a detail sheet. This is where the Presentation layer consumes the domain via server actions.

tour.title: Application & Domain Layer — Server Actions as Repositories
tour.description: JobSync follows DDD: each aggregate has exactly one server-action file acting as its Repository, and they all return the typed `ActionResult<T>` value object rather than raw promises. The Job actions file is the canonical example — list/detail/CRUD plus status-machine transitions and audit — and `ActionResult` defines the response contract every Pattern-A action shares. This layer is the boundary between the UI and the data, and it owns ownership/IDOR enforcement.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript discriminated unions power `ActionResult<T>` — a `success: true` branch carries `data`, the `success: false` branch carries an error, and the compiler forces callers to narrow before accessing either, eliminating a whole class of runtime errors.

tour.title: Domain Models & Aggregate Boundaries
tour.description: Building on the Repository pattern from Step 5, the domain models define the ubiquitous language. The Job model types are among the highest fan-in domain files, and the Person model anchors the independent CRM aggregate root (contacts, interviews, polymorphic tasks/notes) — note how CRM models use a `Crm` prefix to avoid colliding with the legacy Job-centric Note/Task models. These types are the contracts every layer agrees on.

tour.title: The Data Layer — Prisma Schema & Client
tour.description: All persistence flows through a single Prisma client singleton that survives HMR via a `globalThis` cache, and the authoritative data model lives in the Prisma schema — ~40 SQLite-backed entities spanning Job/Automation, the CRM core, enrichment, and notifications. The schema is the highest fan-out node in the graph because almost every aggregate maps onto it. This is the ground truth the domain models in Steps 5-6 are typed against.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma migrations are append-only, ordered SQL files generated from schema diffs. Each one applies incremental, forward-only changes, letting the SQLite database evolve in lockstep with the schema while preserving a replayable history.

tour.title: Connector / Integration ACL — The Shared Kernel
tour.description: External integrations follow the App ↔ Connector ↔ Module Anti-Corruption-Layer pattern. The manifest defines the shared-kernel types every module declares (identity, credentials, health, resilience, i18n), the unified registry stores each registered module with its runtime state, and `register-all` is the side-effect entry point that self-registers every job-discovery, AI-provider, enrichment, and reference-data module. This is how new modules plug in without touching core code.

tour.title: Connector Modules & the Discovery Runner
tour.description: Concrete modules implement the connector interfaces declared in Step 8: the EURES module translates European job-board responses into the domain `DiscoveredVacancy` type, while the Ollama module is an AI-provider module for local resume matching. The enrichment orchestrator runs best-effort fallback chains per dimension (logo, deep-link), and the discovery runner — one of the highest fan-out files — orchestrates a full automation run: search across modules, dedup, enrich, AI match, and stage.

tour.title: Event Bus & Scheduler Coordination
tour.description: The runner and actions don't call each other directly — they communicate through the TypedEventBus, a `globalThis` singleton pub/sub with typed payloads and isolated handler invocation, defined alongside the canonical domain-event types. Time-based and manual automation runs are funneled through the RunCoordinator, the single entry point that enforces a per-automation mutex so a job never double-executes, while the scheduler cron loop queries due automations and dispatches them through it.
tour.languageLesson: The `globalThis` singleton pattern survives Next.js hot-module-reload during development: caching the instance on `globalThis` prevents a new event bus or coordinator from being created on every code change, which would otherwise fragment subscriptions and locks.

tour.title: Multi-Channel Notifications
tour.description: Domain events from Step 10 ultimately surface to users via the notification system. The ChannelRouter is a singleton that fans a single notification draft out to every enabled channel (in-app, email, webhook, push) using a `DispatchContext` that batches all per-user reads into one set of parallel queries. The types file defines the `NotificationChannel` interface each channel implements, making the system extensible without changing the router.

tour.title: Public API v1 — Open Host Service
tour.description: JobSync exposes a versioned REST surface under `/api/v1/*` as a DDD Open Host Service. Every route handler is wrapped by `withApiAuth`, a higher-order function that applies CORS, pre-auth IP rate limiting, API-key authentication, per-key rate limiting, error catching, and security headers in one place — the jobs route is the reference implementation. This wrapper is mandatory: no v1 route may touch Prisma without it.
tour.languageLesson: A higher-order function that wraps route handlers (`withApiAuth(handler)`) centralizes cross-cutting concerns — auth, rate limiting, error sanitization — so each endpoint stays focused on business logic while the security contract is enforced uniformly.

tour.title: Security, GDPR & Data Minimization
tour.description: Security is woven through every layer. NextAuth wires the credentials provider with bcrypt verification and session enrichment; the PII redaction leaf is a dependency-free module that scrubs names/emails/phones before any free text is sent to cloud AI providers (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c)); and the IDOR ownership ADR documents the rule that every Prisma query must filter by `userId`. The GDPR audit report ties these controls to the project's data-protection declarations.

tour.title: Testing, CI & Deployment
tour.description: Finally, see how the code ships safely. The test pyramid is enforced — `account.actions.spec.ts` shows the action-level unit/integration style, and the URL-validation spec guards the SSRF defenses from Step 13. The multi-stage Dockerfile packages the app into a lean runtime image, docker-compose orchestrates it, and the GitHub Actions CI pipeline runs the suite on every push. This is the capstone: every feature in the tour lands behind tests and a reproducible deployment.
tour.languageLesson: Multi-stage Docker builds use separate FROM stages (deps → builder → runner): build-only dependencies stay in the builder stage, and only compiled artifacts are copied into the minimal final image, cutting image size dramatically. CI YAML uses `on` triggers and sequential `steps` to gate merges on a green test run.
